content.text: esult) is a unified cognitive architecture developed by Allen Newell, John Laird, and Paul Rosenbloom at Carnegie Mellon in the mid-1980s. It models human-level cognition as a problem space search: every task is framed as moving from an initial state to a goal state by applying operators.
Unlike BDI (which prescribes a deliberation structure) or neural approaches (which learn weights), SOAR is built around
and a precise five-phase decision cycle. Its
mechanism allows it to learn by compiling experience into new productions, eliminating future subgoal overhead.

attrs.alt: SOAR Architecture: Working Memory, Production Memory, Long-Term Memory with 5-phase decision cycle
attrs.title: SOAR Cognitive Architecture

content.text: Current state, goal stack, operator proposals
Per cycle — cleared on context switch
Every fact in working memory is a
). The id identifies an object; attribute names a property; value is a primitive or another object id. This triple structure enables the Rete algorithm to efficiently detect which productions match.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any


# A Working Memory Element (WME): the triple id, attribute, value
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WME:
    id: str    # object identifier, e.g. "S1" (state), "O1" (operator)
    attr: str  # attribute name, e.g. "type", "name", "value"
    value: Any # string, int, or another WME id

    def __repr__(self):
        return f"({self.id} ^{self.attr} {self.value})"


class WorkingMemory:
    """Rete-based WM: efficiently matches conditions against WMEs."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._wmes: set[WME] = set()

    def add(self, id: str, attr: str, value: Any) -> WME:
        wme = WME(id, attr, value)
        self._wmes.add(wme)
        return wme

    def remove(self, id: str, attr: str, value: Any) -> None:
        self._wmes.discard(WME(id, attr, value))

    def query(self, id: str | None = None, attr: str | None = None,
              value: Any = None) -> list[WME]:
        """Return all WMEs matching the given pattern (None = wildcard)."""
        return [
            w for w in self._wmes
            if (id    is None or w.id    == id)
            and (attr  is None or w.attr  == attr)
            and (value is None or w.value == value)
        ]

    def __repr__(self):
        return "\n".join(str(w) for w in sorted(self._wmes, key=str))


# Example: initialise working memory for a blocks-world task
wm = WorkingMemory()
wm.add("S1", "type",     "state")
wm.add("S1", "top-state", "S1")
wm.add("S1", "goal",      "on-A-B")  # desire: block A on top of block B
wm.add("S1", "on",        "A-table") # current: A is on the table
wm.add("S1", "on",        "B-table") # current: B is on the table
print(wm.query(id="S1", attr="on"))
# [WME(id='S1', attr='on', value='A-table'), WME(id='S1', attr='on', value='B-table')]
is an if-then rule with a left-hand side (LHS) that matches WMEs and a right-hand side (RHS) that adds or removes WMEs. During the
, SOAR fires every production whose LHS matches — this is parallel, not sequential. Productions propose operators, apply preferences, or add derived facts:
— all matching productions fire to quiescence. This adds operator proposals and preference WMEs.
— the architecture resolves operator preferences (required > better > indifferent) and selects one operator. Ties or no proposals →
— the selected operator's application productions fire, modifying WM.
— WM output-link changes are sent as actions to the environment.
When the decision phase cannot select a unique operator, SOAR hits an
. Rather than crashing or returning an error, it automatically creates a
— a new problem space on the goal stack, with the impasse itself as the problem to solve. This is
: every reasoning difficulty becomes a new problem-solving episode.
— no operator was proposed. Agent must find or create one.
— multiple equally preferred operators proposed. Agent must differentiate them.
— operators with contradictory preferences. Agent must resolve the conflict.
— preference contradiction within a single operator. Agent must remove the constraint.
When a subgoal is resolved, SOAR's

automatically creates a new production that encodes the result. The next time the same situation arises, the new production fires directly in the elaboration phase — bypassing the subgoal entirely. This is the only learning mechanism in vanilla SOAR:

class ChunkingLearner:
    """
    Demonstrates the principle behind SOAR chunking.

    Real chunking traces the WM elements that justified each step
    of subgoal resolution and builds a production that maps
    similar initial conditions directly to the same conclusion,
    bypassing the subgoal on future encounters.
    """

    def __init__(self):
        self.chunks: list[Production] = []
        self.subgoal_log: list[tuple] = []  # (conditions_snapshot, result_action)

    def record_subgoal(self, conditions: dict, result_action: str) -> None:
        """Record what conditions led to what result during subgoal resolution."""
        self.subgoal_log.append((conditions, result_action))

    def compile_chunk(self) -> Production | None:
        """Turn last subgoal trace into a new production."""
        if not self.subgoal_log:
            return None
        conds, action = self.subgoal_log[-1]

        # Build a closure that matches the same conditions
        frozen_conds = dict(conds)
        frozen_action = action

        def chunk_lhs(wm: WorkingMemory) -> bool:
            return all(
                bool(wm.query(attr=k, value=v))
                for k, v in frozen_conds.items()
            )

        def chunk_rhs(wm: WorkingMemory) -> None:
            wm.add("O_chunk", "name", frozen_action)
            wm.add("S1", "operator", "O_chunk")

        chunk = Production(f"chunk-{len(self.chunks)+1}", chunk_lhs, chunk_rhs)
        self.chunks.append(chunk)
        print(f"New chunk compiled: {chunk.name} → {frozen_action}")
        return chunk


# Simulate: agent learns from subgoal and avoids it next time
learner = ChunkingLearner()
learner.record_subgoal({"goal": "on-A-B", "on": "A-table"}, "move-A-to-B")
new_prod = learner.compile_chunk()
# "New chunk compiled: chunk-1 → move-A-to-B"

# Install chunk into a fresh agent
agent2 = SOARAgent()
agent2.pm.add(new_prod)  # no subgoal needed now!
result = agent2.decide({"goal": "on-A-B", "on": "A-table"})
print(result)  # move-A-to-B  (direct, no impasse)
: the system derives a general rule from the specific reasoning trace, generalising over the variables that did not affect the outcome.
system that handles both fast reflexes (production matching) and deliberate reasoning (subgoaling) in one framework.
without human labelling — chunking compiles experience automatically.
with well-defined operators and state representations.
to tasks requiring gradient-based learning, continuous perception (vision, audio), or massive state spaces — modern practice augments SOAR with neural sub-symbolic perception layers.

SOAR frames all cognition as problem space search: states are WME triples, operators propose transitions, and the five-phase decision cycle selects and applies one operator per tick. When the architecture cannot decide, universal subgoaling creates a new problem to solve the impasse. Chunking converts subgoal reasoning into compiled productions, eliminating future overhead and serving as the system's learning mechanism. SOAR has been applied to large-scale intelligent agents in games, simulation, robotics, and natural language processing over four decades of active development.
